---
title: "Collection Modules Walkthrough"
author: Phil Henrickson
format:
  html:
    code-fold: false
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    embed-resources: true
execute:
  enabled: true
jupyter:
  kernelspec:
    name: bgg-predictive-models
    language: python
    display_name: Python 3 (bgg-predictive-models .venv)
---

Step-by-step walkthrough of the refactored `src/collection/` modules after the
collection-modules branch. Each section exercises one piece of the pipeline
end-to-end against a real user collection, so we can see shapes, distributions,
and model behaviour at each stage before wiring the full CLI loop.

Run cells top-to-bottom. Set `USERNAME` below to the BGG user you want to walk
through.

```{python}
#| tags: [setup]
USERNAME = "GOBBluth89"
```
```{python}
#| tags: [setup]
import os
import sys
from pathlib import Path

# Ensure the project root is on sys.path AND is the working directory so
# `src.*` imports work and relative paths in .env (e.g.
# GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS=credentials/service-account-key.json)
# resolve when Quarto runs from the notebooks/ directory.
PROJECT_ROOT = Path().resolve()
if (PROJECT_ROOT / "src").is_dir():
    ROOT = PROJECT_ROOT
else:
    ROOT = PROJECT_ROOT.parent
sys.path.insert(0, str(ROOT))
os.chdir(ROOT)

import logging
logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO, format="%(levelname)s %(name)s: %(message)s")

import polars as pl
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
```

## 1. Load a collection

Fetch the raw collection for `USERNAME` straight from the BGG XML API via
`BGGCollectionLoader`. This is the outcome-agnostic raw snapshot.

```{python}
from src.collection.collection_loader import BGGCollectionLoader

loader = BGGCollectionLoader(USERNAME)
raw_collection = loader.get_collection()
print(f"rows: {raw_collection.height}")
print(f"columns ({len(raw_collection.columns)}): {raw_collection.columns}")
raw_collection.head(5)
```

### Quick sanity checks

```{python}
# Status flag counts
for col in ["owned", "previously_owned", "want", "wishlist", "preordered", "for_trade"]:
    if col in raw_collection.columns:
        n = raw_collection.filter(pl.col(col) == True).height
        print(f"{col}: {n}")

# Rating coverage
rated_df = raw_collection.filter(pl.col("user_rating") > 0)
print(f"\nrated (user_rating > 0): {rated_df.height}")
if rated_df.height > 0:
    print(f"mean rating: {rated_df['user_rating'].mean():.2f}")
else:
    print("mean rating: (no rated games)")
```

---

## 2. Store a collection

Upsert the raw snapshot into `collections.user_collections`. The table is
managed by Terraform (`terraform/bigquery.tf` + `terraform/schemas/user_collections.json`)
so the Python code never creates it — if the table is missing, run
`terraform apply` from the `terraform/` directory.

Each pull MERGEs against the existing state for this user: new games are
inserted, existing games are updated in place, and games that have disappeared
from the collection are soft-deleted via `removed_at`. Re-pulling the same
data is a no-op (idempotent).

```{python}
from src.collection.collection_storage import CollectionStorage

bq_storage = CollectionStorage(environment="dev")
bq_storage.save_collection(USERNAME, raw_collection)

reloaded = bq_storage.get_latest_collection(USERNAME)
print(f"active rows: {reloaded.height if reloaded is not None else 0}")
```

### Inspect lifecycle timestamps

```{python}
if reloaded is not None:
    foo = reloaded.select(["game_id", "game_name", "first_seen_at", "updated_at"])
    print(foo)
```

---

## 3. Process + split the collection

Run the full data-prep chain: process → apply_outcome → split. Start with the
`own` outcome as the concrete example. The processor canonicalizes BGG column
names (e.g. `previously_owned` → `prev_owned`) and joins with the game universe.

```{python}
from src.utils.config import load_config
from src.collection.collection_processor import CollectionProcessor, ProcessorConfig
from src.collection.outcomes import load_outcomes, apply_outcome

project_config = load_config()
bq_config = project_config.get_bigquery_config()

processor = CollectionProcessor(
    config=bq_config,
    environment="dev",
    processor_config=ProcessorConfig(
        use_predicted_complexity=True,
        use_embeddings=False,
    ),
)
processed = processor.process(USERNAME)
print(f"processed: {processed.height} rows, {len(processed.columns)} columns")
print(f"has prev_owned: {'prev_owned' in processed.columns}")
processed.head(3)
```

Load the five outcomes declared in `config.yaml`:

```{python}
outcomes = load_outcomes(project_config.raw_config)
for name, outcome in outcomes.items():
    print(f"{name}: task={outcome.task}, rule={type(outcome.label_rule).__name__}, require={outcome.require}")
```

Apply one outcome — `own` — to get a labeled dataframe:

```{python}
labeled_own = apply_outcome(processed, outcomes["own"])
print(f"labeled rows: {labeled_own.height}")
print(f"positives (label=True): {labeled_own.filter(pl.col('label') == True).height}")
print(f"negatives (label=False): {labeled_own.filter(pl.col('label') == False).height}")
```

### Load the game universe and create a splitter

Join with universe of BGG games

```{python}
from src.data.loader import BGGDataLoader
from src.collection.collection_split import (
    CollectionSplitter,
    ClassificationSplitConfig,
    RegressionSplitConfig,
)

universe_loader = BGGDataLoader(bq_config)
universe_df = universe_loader.load_training_data(min_ratings=0, end_train_year = 2030)
print(f"universe: {universe_df.height} games, {len(universe_df.columns)} columns")
```

Use the collection splitter

```{python}
# Time-based split: train on everything through 2022, validate on
# 2023-2024, test on 2025. Anything 2026+ is "upcoming" and gets scored
# separately from score_universe.
splitter = CollectionSplitter(
    universe_df=universe_df,
    classification_config=ClassificationSplitConfig(
        split_mode="time_based",
        train_through=2022,
        prediction_window=2,  # val = 2023-2024
        test_window=1,  # test = 2025
        random_seed=42,
    ),
    regression_config=RegressionSplitConfig(random_seed=42),
)
train_df, val_df, test_df = splitter.split(labeled_own, outcomes["own"])
print(f"train: {train_df.height}, val: {val_df.height}, test: {test_df.height}")
for name, d in [("train", train_df), ("val", val_df), ("test", test_df)]:
    if d.height and "year_published" in d.columns:
        print(
            f"  {name}: year range {d['year_published'].min()}-{d['year_published'].max()}"
        )
print(f"train columns ({len(train_df.columns)}): {train_df.columns[:15]}...")
```

---

## 4. Examine split distributions across outcomes

Loop every outcome, label it, split it, and report positive rate + split sizes.
Sanity-check that `stratified_random` preserves the label ratio and that
`time_based` cuts by year.

```{python}
def describe_split(name: str, train, val, test, task: str):
    rows = []
    for split_name, df in [("train", train), ("val", val), ("test", test)]:
        if df.height == 0:
            rows.append({"outcome": name, "split": split_name, "rows": 0})
            continue
        row = {"outcome": name, "split": split_name, "rows": df.height}
        if task == "classification":
            pos = df.filter(pl.col("label") == True).height
            row["pos"] = pos
            row["pos_rate"] = pos / df.height if df.height else 0.0
        else:  # regression
            row["label_mean"] = df["label"].mean()
            row["label_std"] = df["label"].std()
        if "year_published" in df.columns:
            row["year_min"] = df["year_published"].min()
            row["year_max"] = df["year_published"].max()
        rows.append(row)
    return rows

all_rows = []
for name, outcome in outcomes.items():
    labeled = apply_outcome(processed, outcome)
    try:
        tr, va, te = splitter.split(labeled, outcome)
    except ValueError as e:
        print(f"skipping {name}: {e}")
        continue
    all_rows.extend(describe_split(name, tr, va, te, outcome.task))

pd.DataFrame(all_rows)
```

### Time-based split

Switch the classification splitter to `time_based` mode and re-split `own` to
see how the year cutoff carves the positives.

```{python}
time_splitter = CollectionSplitter(
    universe_df=universe_df,
    classification_config=ClassificationSplitConfig(
        split_mode="time_based",
        train_through=2022,
        prediction_window=2,   # val = 2023-2024
        test_window=2,         # test = 2025-2026
        min_ratings_for_negatives=0,
        random_seed=42,
    ),
)

own_tb_train, own_tb_val, own_tb_test = time_splitter.split(labeled_own, outcomes["own"])

def year_ranges(df, label_val):
    d = df.filter(pl.col("label") == label_val)
    if d.height == 0:
        return "(empty)"
    return f"{d['year_published'].min()}-{d['year_published'].max()}, n={d.height}"

print("positives:")
print(f"  train: {year_ranges(own_tb_train, True)}")
print(f"  val:   {year_ranges(own_tb_val, True)}")
print(f"  test:  {year_ranges(own_tb_test, True)}")
print("negatives:")
print(f"  train: {year_ranges(own_tb_train, False)}")
print(f"  val:   {year_ranges(own_tb_val, False)}")
print(f"  test:  {year_ranges(own_tb_test, False)}")
```

---

## 5. Build the sklearn pipeline

Train `own` via `CollectionModel`. The returned pipeline is a
`sklearn.pipeline.Pipeline` with a preprocessor step and a model step.

```{python}
from src.collection.collection_model import (
    CollectionModel,
    ClassificationModelConfig,
)
from src.collection.collection_split import downsample_negatives

model = CollectionModel(
    username=USERNAME,
    outcome=outcomes["own"],
    classification_config=ClassificationModelConfig(
        # model_type defaults to "logistic"; flip to "lightgbm" or "catboost" to try others.
        tuning_metric="log_loss",
        patience=5,
    ),
)

# Inspect the unfit pipeline before training.
unfit_pipeline = model.build_pipeline()
print(unfit_pipeline)

# Optional: downsample training-set negatives to 5 per positive for class balance.
# Only touch the training frame — val and test keep the real class distribution
# so tuning and evaluation stay honest.
train_down = downsample_negatives(train_df, ratio=100, random_seed=1999)
print(
    f"train before: {train_df.height} rows "
    f"({train_df.filter(pl.col('label') == True).height} pos); "
    f"train after: {train_down.height} rows "
    f"({train_down.filter(pl.col('label') == True).height} pos)"
)
```


Now fit the pipeline

```{python}

pipeline_obj, best_params = model.train(train_down, val_df)
print("pipeline steps:", [s[0] for s in pipeline_obj.steps])
print("best params:", best_params)
print("done")

```
---

## 6. Inspect preprocessing

Look at what the preprocessor produces: what columns come out, which get
transformed, and how many features the model actually trains on.

```{python}
X_transformed_df = model.transform_features(pipeline_obj, train_down)
print(f"post-preprocess features: {X_transformed_df.shape[1]}")
print("first 30:", list(X_transformed_df.columns[:30]))
X_transformed_df.head()
```

---

## 7. Train + evaluate

Training already happened above; evaluate on validation and test.

Classification models pick a decision threshold on val (optimizing the
configured metric, e.g. F2) and then use that same threshold to report
test metrics. Evaluating at 0.5 on an imbalanced problem collapses
precision/recall/F1 to ~0 even when the probability ranking is fine
(AUC / PR-AUC stay sensible) — always pass the optimized threshold.

```{python}
threshold = model.find_threshold(pipeline_obj, val_df)
print(f"optimized threshold ({model.classification_config.threshold_optimization_metric}): {threshold:.4f}")
```

```{python}
val_metrics = model.evaluate(pipeline_obj, val_df, threshold=threshold)
test_metrics = model.evaluate(pipeline_obj, test_df, threshold=threshold)

pd.DataFrame({"val": val_metrics, "test": test_metrics}).round(4)
```

### Stratified by `users_rated`

Overall metrics can look rosy when the negative pool is dominated by
obscure games the model rarely needs to separate out. Break val and test
metrics down by `users_rated` (default buckets: <25, 25–99, 100+) to see
how the model actually behaves on games people have heard of.

```{python}
val_stratified = model.evaluate_stratified(pipeline_obj, val_df, threshold=threshold)
test_stratified = model.evaluate_stratified(pipeline_obj, test_df, threshold=threshold)

print("val stratified:")
print(val_stratified.round(4))
print("\ntest stratified:")
print(test_stratified.round(4))
```

---

## 8. Extract model quantities

Pull feature importances (for tree models) or coefficients (for logistic) out
of the trained pipeline, aligned with the preprocessor's output feature names.

```{python}
importance = model.feature_importance(pipeline_obj, train_down)
importance.head(20)
```

---

## 9. Top games per split

Rank games by predicted `own` probability within each split. The
`model.top_games()` call handles scoring, optional exclusion of owned
games, and sorting — returns a tidy polars DataFrame.

```{python}
owned_ids = raw_collection.filter(pl.col("owned") == True)["game_id"].to_list()

score_universe = processor.load_features()
upcoming = score_universe.filter(pl.col("year_published") >= 2026)
print(f"score universe: {score_universe.height}, upcoming (2026+): {upcoming.height}")
```

### Top 25 unowned games in the (downsampled) training set

```{python}
pl.Config.set_tbl_cols(-1)
pl.Config.set_tbl_rows(50)
pl.Config.set_fmt_str_lengths(200)
      # show up to 50 rows
model.top_games(pipeline_obj, train_down, n=25, exclude_game_ids=owned_ids)
```

### Top 25 games in the validation set (2023–2024)

```{python}
model.top_games(pipeline_obj, val_df, n=25)
```

### Top 25 games in the test set (2025)

```{python}
model.top_games(pipeline_obj, test_df, n=25)
```

### Top 25 upcoming games (2026+)

```{python}
model.top_games(pipeline_obj, upcoming, n=25)
```



```{python}

model.top_games(pipeline_obj, upcoming, n=500).filter(pl.col("name")=='Market Fresh')

```
---


## Next

Once the checklist is green, hook the same steps into `CollectionPipeline.run_full_pipeline`
and make sure the CLI round-trip (`make train-collection USERNAME=... OUTCOME=own`)
matches what we see here.
